name: Net Impact Accounting → AI Infrastructure Transparency → Informed Climate Policy
investmentCase: No entity tracks whether AI infrastructure is net-positive or net-negative for decarbonization. $200B+/year in hyperscaler capex creates demand while ~$50–100M in fragmented demand-side measurement covers only half the ledger. The net-impact synthesis hasn't been built because (a) the companies that generate the data benefit from opacity and (b) even perfect data may not drive policy — climate information has been clear for decades without adequate response.
investableInsight: Phase 1 (~$2–5M, 1–2 years): Fund foundational methodology. Two unsolved prerequisites block credible net-impact accounting: (1) Counterfactual modeling — build peer-reviewed scenario frameworks answering 'what would grid emissions look like without AI infrastructure?' with transparent, contestable assumptions. (2) Workload isolation — develop standardized protocols for attributing energy consumption to AI vs. non-AI workloads within mixed-use facilities. Both are tractable academic-scale problems that can be developed using publicly available data (IEA, Climate TRACE satellite monitoring, grid operator records) without requiring corporate cooperation. Phase 2 (builds on Phase 1): Fund the net-impact dashboard/accounting framework — now with credible methodology underpinning it. Phase 2 requires either mandatory disclosure regulations or independent estimation methods robust enough to work without corporate data. Political economy constraint: The companies that hold the most granular data (hyperscalers) benefit from opacity. Voluntary disclosure has failed for 20+ years in climate accounting. Phase 1 can proceed without corporate cooperation; Phase 2 likely cannot without regulatory compulsion (EU CBAM context, SEC climate disclosure precedent) or satellite/grid-level estimation methods that bypass corporate reporting entirely.
chainNarrative: This cross-cutting chain claims that building transparent net-impact accounting for AI infrastructure — measuring both AI's grid optimization benefits and its own energy demand — can provide the evidence base needed for informed climate policy on AI growth. Evidence is strong at Step 1: AI energy demand data exists (IEA, hyperscaler disclosures, Climate TRACE) and grid optimization benefits are documented in pilots. The chain breaks at Step 2: no entity synthesizes these into a net-impact framework, and corporate disclosures use inconsistent methodologies (RECs vs. physical delivery, scope variations). Step 3 — policy action based on net-impact data — is entirely unmeasured.
keyConfounders: Corporate disclosure inconsistency (RECs vs. physical delivery), rapidly changing AI compute efficiency (inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022), regional grid mix variation making global averages misleading, hyperscaler resistance to transparent reporting, Jevons paradox (efficiency gains consumed by demand growth). CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Political economy of disclosure — the companies that would need to provide data are the same companies that benefit from opacity. Voluntary disclosure has failed for 20+ years in climate accounting; mandatory requirements face industry lobbying. (2) Even perfect accounting may not drive policy — climate data has been clear for decades; the bottleneck is political will, not information quality. (3) Counterfactual impossibility — calculating 'net' impact requires modeling what the grid would look like without AI, a counterfactual that is methodologically intractable and assumption-dependent. (4) Scope boundary problem — isolating AI-specific energy demand from non-AI compute in mixed data center workloads is an unsolved methodological problem (IEA, academic researchers confirm this). (5) Moving target — AI compute efficiency changes so rapidly that any published framework would be outdated within months.

name: AI Operations → Nonprofit/NGO Effectiveness → Amplified Mission Impact
impactEstimate: ~10M nonprofits globally. If AI improves operational effectiveness by 10% (fundraising, grant writing, reporting, program delivery), aggregate impact across the social sector could redirect billions in currently wasted capacity toward mission delivery.
investmentCase: Unfunded because no single nonprofit's 10% improvement makes headlines. The aggregation claim (many small improvements = transformational) is plausible but untested — and the IT revolution precedent is discouraging: previous technology waves improved nonprofit operations without producing measurable aggregate mission impact.
investableInsight: Fund a controlled study across 50-100 nonprofits measuring: (1) whether AI operational tools produce measurable improvements in mission outcomes (not just operational efficiency), (2) whether saved time goes to mission delivery vs. more admin/meetings/scope creep, and (3) whether the IT revolution precedent holds — does AI break the pattern of operational improvement without mission impact?
chainNarrative: This chain claims that AI tools for operations (fundraising, grant writing, reporting, donor management) can make nonprofits and NGOs meaningfully more effective, compounding across ~10M organizations globally into transformational aggregate impact. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI demonstrably improves individual operational tasks (grant writing speed, donor segmentation). The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no study measures whether AI-driven operational improvements translate to better mission outcomes, and the aggregation claim (many small improvements = transformational) is entirely unmeasured.
keyConfounders: Nonprofit technology adoption barriers, digital divide across org sizes, operational improvement ≠ mission improvement, AI tool costs relative to nonprofit budgets, measurement difficulty for mission outcomes. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Operational efficiency ≠ mission effectiveness is a well-documented problem in nonprofit management (Kaplan 2001). Organizations often redirect saved time to more admin, meetings, or scope creep — not to mission delivery. The chain assumes a direct link that nonprofit management literature has shown doesn't hold without deliberate strategy. (2) The IT revolution already tested this hypothesis — computers, email, CRM systems, and cloud tools all improved nonprofit operations without producing measurable aggregate mission impact. AI may follow the same pattern: real operational improvement, no measurable mission change. (3) AI tools may homogenize nonprofit strategies — if every organization uses AI for grant writing and donor management, proposals and strategies converge, reducing the diversity of approaches that makes the social sector resilient. Funders may see identical AI-written proposals rather than authentic organizational voices.

name: AI Optimization → Metric Gaming → Mission Drift
impactEstimate: Goodhart's Law applied to AI optimization: when organizations use AI to optimize measurable outcomes, they systematically shift resources toward what's measurable rather than what matters. Affects every sector where AI-driven metrics guide decisions — healthcare quality scores, educational test scores, climate offset credits, employment placement rates.
investmentCase: Unfunded because AI optimization is embedded in the commercial AI market — every AI dashboard and analytics tool drives metric gaming as a side effect. The causing activity ($211B+ AI market) is heavily funded; measuring whether metric improvements reflect real impact is unfunded. Organizations are deploying AI optimization without measuring whether it's improving outcomes or just improving metrics — automating Goodhart's Law at scale.
investableInsight: Fund a cross-sector study comparing AI-optimized vs. traditionally-managed programs on BOTH metric improvement AND independent outcome assessment, quantifying the gap between the two. Use existing data (IRS 990s, program reports, government performance data). Additionally: build independent outcome assessment infrastructure that operates outside AI-optimized metric systems — measuring what matters, not just what's measured. This is one of the more actionable investable insights in the gap map.
chainNarrative: This chain identifies how AI optimization of measurable outcomes causes organizations to drift toward what's measurable rather than what matters — Goodhart's Law at institutional scale. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — metric gaming is well-documented across sectors (teaching to the test, hospital readmission gaming, police crime stat manipulation). The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: whether AI accelerates metric gaming beyond historical levels is unmeasured, and the net effect (AI-driven improvement minus AI-driven gaming) is unknown.
keyConfounders: Pre-existing metric gaming independent of AI, difficulty defining 'what matters' vs. what's measured, organizational incentives aligned with metrics not outcomes, auditor capacity to detect gaming. Chain-negating (potential): AI might IMPROVE outcome measurement, not just proxy gaming — if AI can measure previously-unmeasurable outcomes (sentiment analysis, satellite imagery, etc.), it could shift organizations toward better metrics. Counter: creating better measures is a deliberate expensive activity; gaming existing proxies is the default that happens automatically. Organizational awareness of Goodhart's Law is increasing, which may partially self-correct.

name: AI Dependency → Institutional Deskilling → Reduced Organizational Resilience
impactEstimate: Organizations across all sectors risk losing internal expertise as AI handles more functions. When tools fail, change pricing, or get discontinued, organizations without preserved institutional knowledge face operational crises — analogous to outsourcing dependency but more pervasive.
investmentCase: Unfunded because the harm is invisible until it materializes. Organizations are enthusiastically adopting AI (72% adoption rate) without measuring institutional knowledge loss, creating fragility that accumulates silently — like structural damage before an earthquake. The causing activity ($211B+ AI investment) is heavily funded; the measurement of downstream harm is unfunded.
investableInsight: Study organizations that have already experienced AI tool disruptions (pricing changes, vendor pivots, outages) and measure their coping capacity vs. less AI-dependent peers. Natural experiments > planned longitudinal studies for this chain — the disruption event is what reveals the fragility.
chainNarrative: This chain identifies how organizations that over-rely on AI tools lose internal expertise, creating fragility when tools fail, change, or get priced up. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — IT outsourcing literature documents institutional deskilling from technology dependency. The chain is entirely speculative at Steps 2-3: no measurement of AI-specific institutional deskilling exists, and the rate and severity of resilience loss depends on which functions organizations delegate to AI.
keyConfounders: AI tools may become so reliable that retained expertise is unnecessary, organizational adaptation to tool changes, variation in dependency depth across functions, competitive pressure to adopt regardless of risks. Chain-negating: AI-as-infrastructure argument — if AI becomes as reliable as electricity, 'deskilling' is just modernization, not fragility. Counter: aviation autopilot is also reliable infrastructure, and automation complacency still kills people. Organizational adaptation speed varies. Market competition may create demand for AI-resilient organizations.

name: AI Evidence Synthesis → Better Decision-Making → More Effective Resource Allocation
impactEstimate: ~$500B+ in annual philanthropic and development spending globally. If AI evidence synthesis improved resource allocation decisions by even 5%, that's $25B+ redirected to higher-impact interventions annually.
investmentCase: Unfunded because AI synthesis solves the wrong problem. Evidence-based policy has been a field for 30+ years and evidence uptake remains unsolved. Making synthesis faster is like giving a faster printer to someone who doesn't read reports. The bottleneck is institutional incentives, not information availability — and no one is investing in the evidence-to-decision pipeline.
investableInsight: Fund a randomized pilot providing AI-synthesized evidence briefings to 20-30 funders, measuring actual allocation shifts (not just satisfaction). Must be honest that GiveWell took years to build evidence culture — a briefing pilot may show null results even if the concept has long-term potential. The pilot must measure behavior change, not information quality.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can synthesize research across fields faster than humans, enabling funders, policymakers, and program designers to make better resource allocation decisions with compound effects across all domains. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — tools like Semantic Scholar and Elicit demonstrate cross-domain synthesis capability. The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no evidence that decision-makers change behavior based on AI-synthesized evidence, and institutional incentives (political considerations, donor preferences, risk aversion) often override evidence quality in allocation decisions.
keyConfounders: Decision-maker incentives override evidence quality, political and donor preference constraints, AI hallucination risk in synthesis (5-15%), institutional inertia in allocation processes, difficulty measuring counterfactual allocation quality. Chain-negating: evidence uptake is the bottleneck, not evidence availability — 30+ years of evidence-based policy research shows decision-makers don't change behavior based on better evidence alone. Making synthesis faster doesn't address the actual problem. Selection bias in AI-accessible evidence (skews toward English-language, Global North research).

name: AI Capacity Building → Smaller Orgs Compete → Democratized Impact
impactEstimate: ~90% of nonprofits have budgets under $1M with limited staff capacity. AI could enable small organizations to perform functions (data analysis, program evaluation, communications, compliance) that previously required dedicated specialists, potentially unlocking capacity equivalent to adding 1-2 FTEs per organization.
investmentCase: Unfunded because the 'AI democratizes everything' narrative is too attractive to test. No one is measuring whether AI adoption by organization size is narrowing or widening the capacity gap. Historical tech adoption patterns suggest AI may widen inequality, but the direction is genuinely unknown — making this a rare case where a tracking study is the correct first investment.
investableInsight: Fund a comparative tracking study ($200K-500K) measuring AI adoption and capacity outcomes by organization size (<$1M, $1-10M, >$10M budgets) over 2 years, specifically testing whether AI is narrowing or widening the organizational capacity gap. This is one of the few cases where 'fund a study' is the right answer — the direction of the effect is unknown and determines whether this chain is a benefit or a harm.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI levels the playing field for small NGOs, local governments, and community organizations that can't afford specialist staff. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI tools demonstrably perform tasks that previously required specialists (data analysis, communications, compliance). The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no evidence that small organizations actually adopt and effectively use AI tools, digital divide and AI literacy barriers may widen rather than narrow the capacity gap, and incumbent large organizations may benefit more from AI than small ones.
keyConfounders: Digital divide and AI literacy barriers, cost of AI tools relative to small org budgets, incumbent advantage for large organizations, change management capacity, dependence on tech platforms that may change pricing. Chain-negating: incumbent advantage — historical tech adoption patterns (PCs, internet, enterprise SaaS) consistently show larger organizations adopt first and benefit more. AI could widen the capacity gap it claims to narrow. AI tool pricing trajectory (current low prices may be acquisition strategy). Platform dependency and vendor lock-in for small orgs with no negotiating power.

name: AI Translation → Reduced Language Barriers → Global Knowledge Access
impactEstimate: ~7,000 languages globally, ~40% of world population lacks access to information in their primary language. AI translation could unlock access to health information, legal aid, education materials, and climate data for billions — but quality degrades sharply for low-resource languages.
investmentCase: Commercially funded for high-resource languages (Google, DeepL, Meta NLLB) but critically underfunded for low-resource languages — the populations most in need have the worst quality. Investment Level corrected to Minimal/None per weakest-link rule (Step 3 has zero investment in measuring whether translation changes outcomes).
investableInsight: Fund development and evaluation of AI translation for the 20 highest-impact low-resource languages, measuring three things: (1) translation quality improvement, (2) whether access to translated content changes knowledge uptake and service utilization, AND (3) mistranslation rates and consequences in high-stakes domains (health, legal). Include harm measurement alongside benefit measurement.
chainNarrative: This chain claims real-time AI translation can remove language as a barrier to accessing services, research, and governance across every domain. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI translation quality for high-resource language pairs approaches human parity. The chain weakens sharply at Steps 2-3: quality degrades for low-resource languages (the ones most needed), no study measures whether translation access actually changes knowledge uptake or service utilization, and cultural adaptation beyond literal translation remains unsolved.
keyConfounders: Low-resource language training data scarcity, cultural context beyond literal translation, digital literacy and access barriers, institutional willingness to use AI-translated materials, liability for mistranslation in health/legal contexts. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Information access ≠ knowledge uptake — even when translation exists, people may not use translated materials if they don't trust the source, lack digital literacy, or rely on oral traditions and community knowledge. Removing the language barrier is necessary but not sufficient. (2) Mistranslation risk in high-stakes domains — AI translation errors in health, legal, or safety contexts could cause direct harm. For low-resource languages with poor quality, deploying translation could be worse than no translation — bad medical advice is more dangerous than no advice. (3) Commercial incentives work against equity — AI translation investment follows market size. Low-resource languages have small markets, ensuring the populations most in need will always be last in line without dedicated philanthropic intervention.

name: AI Monitoring → Real-Time Accountability → Reduced Waste/Corruption
impactEstimate: World Bank estimates $1-2.6T lost to corruption annually in developing countries. AI monitoring of spending patterns, aid delivery, and program outcomes could flag anomalies in real-time, potentially recovering or preventing billions in waste.
investmentCase: Unfunded because the investment gap reflects an incentive gap, not an oversight. Private sector fraud detection is Heavy because banks lose money from fraud. Public sector corruption lacks analogous enforcement incentives — the 'victims' (citizens, aid recipients) don't control the enforcement mechanism. AI monitoring technology exists but no one has aligned the enforcement incentives needed to make detection actionable.
investableInsight: Fund monitoring pilots specifically in contexts with independent anti-corruption institutions (countries with functional audit offices), paired with enforcement linkage, measuring enforcement actions — not just detection accuracy. Don't deploy in highest-corruption contexts first — start where enforcement is possible. A pure monitoring pilot without enforcement linkage will produce detection without action.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-driven monitoring of government spending, aid delivery, and program outcomes can reduce waste and corruption across public and nonprofit sectors globally. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI anomaly detection in financial transactions is well-established in the private sector. The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: applying monitoring to public sector and aid delivery faces political resistance, no evidence that AI monitoring produces accountability (detection ≠ enforcement), and monitoring without enforcement capacity may simply document waste without reducing it.
keyConfounders: Political resistance to transparency, enforcement capacity gaps, data quality and availability in developing countries, monitoring fatigue, corruption adaptation to detection methods. Chain-negating: political economy of enforcement — the actors who control enforcement mechanisms are often the beneficiaries of corruption. AI monitoring can produce perfect detection and still achieve nothing if the enforcement step is controlled by those being monitored. Data access restrictions by corrupt actors. Surveillance risk — monitoring infrastructure could be repurposed for political surveillance. Gaming — sophisticated actors learn to structure corruption to avoid AI detection patterns.

name: AI Literacy Programs → Informed Adoption → Preserved Institutional Knowledge
impactEstimate: If effective AI literacy programs reach organizations across all sectors, they could preserve institutional knowledge while enabling AI adoption — addressing the deskilling and metric gaming harms simultaneously. Analogous to cybersecurity training: necessary but underfunded organizational capacity building.
investmentCase: Unfunded because AI literacy investment is concentrated on individual tool proficiency, not organizational capacity preservation. No program teaches organizations how to adopt AI while preserving institutional knowledge — and even if such programs existed, the evidence-based policy literature on training-vs-incentives strongly suggests structural incentives matter more than education alone.
investableInsight: Test whether AI literacy + structural incentives (e.g., funder requirements for demonstrating institutional knowledge preservation) outperforms literacy alone. The comparison shouldn't be 'training vs. no training' — it should be 'training only vs. training + incentive design.' Training alone is unlikely to withstand cost pressures pushing toward over-delegation.
chainNarrative: This chain proposes that structured AI literacy programs can help organizations adopt AI without losing core competencies, mitigating both institutional deskilling and metric gaming risks. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — organizational training programs demonstrably change technology adoption patterns (cybersecurity awareness programs reduce incidents 40-60%). The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: no AI-specific organizational literacy program has been rigorously evaluated, and whether training changes adoption behavior in practice (vs. just awareness) is unmeasured.
keyConfounders: Training effectiveness decay over time, rapid AI capability changes outpacing curricula, organizational incentives to adopt fast rather than carefully, variation in organizational readiness, cost barriers for small organizations. Chain-negating: cost pressures and competitive dynamics override training — even organizations that understand the risks of AI over-delegation may still do it because competitors are, funders expect efficiency, and AI produces faster/cheaper outputs. Same structural weakness as seatbelt education before seatbelt laws. Selection bias in who trains (orgs already concerned about AI risks seek training; those who need it most won't).

name: AI Matching → Labor Market Efficiency → Reduced Unemployment
impactEstimate: ~200M unemployed globally (ILO). Frictional unemployment accounts for 1-3% of workforce. If AI reduces job search duration by 20-30%, significant individual benefit but moderate systemic impact.
investmentCase: Weakest chain in Labor domain — commits a category error conflating frictional (1–3%) with structural unemployment. Even perfect matching has a small ceiling. Algorithmic bias risks making matching worse for marginalized groups. Deprioritize relative to displacement and creation chains.
investableInsight: Fund a natural experiment comparing labor markets with high vs. low AI matching platform adoption, measuring job search duration, match quality (tenure, wage), and aggregate frictional unemployment rates. Scope limitation: this study can only address frictional unemployment (1–3% of workforce). Claims about structural unemployment should be removed from this chain. Bias audit: include demographic stratification to detect whether AI matching amplifies or reduces hiring discrimination. Note: deprioritize relative to other Labor chains with larger impact ceilings.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-powered job matching reduces frictional unemployment by extracting better signals from resumes and job requirements. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI matching tools are widely deployed and report faster hiring. The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: no rigorous study has measured AI's effect on aggregate frictional unemployment, and structural unemployment (driven by deep skills mismatches) cannot be solved by better matching alone.
keyConfounders: Algorithmic hiring bias, match speed vs. match quality tradeoff, demand-side unemployment factors, geographic and skills barriers, structural vs. frictional unemployment distinction. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Category error — Step 3 claims matching solves structural unemployment, but structural unemployment means jobs don't exist for available skill profiles. Better matching can't solve a demand-side problem. This is a logical flaw in the chain's construction. (2) Algorithmic hiring bias — AI matching tools trained on historical hiring data reproduce and amplify existing biases (gender, race, age, disability). 'Better matching' may mean 'faster replication of existing discrimination.' (3) Selection bias — platform users are not representative of the unemployed population. Better-positioned candidates self-select, inflating apparent effectiveness. (4) Match speed vs. quality tradeoff — faster hiring may increase mismatch (shorter tenure, lower wages).

name: AI Automation → Job Displacement → Economic Inequality
impactEstimate: Goldman Sachs: 300M jobs globally exposed to AI automation. IMF: 40% of jobs globally exposed, 60% in advanced economies. McKinsey: 400-800M workers could need to change occupations by 2030. Even partial displacement affects hundreds of millions.
investmentCase: Critically under-tracked — $109B/year drives AI development (creating displacement as a side effect) while <$1B tracks the effects. The speed differential is chain-negating: AI compresses decades of automation into years while safety nets remain designed for slow transitions. 2026 may be the last clean baseline window.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal cohort study tracking workers in AI-exposed occupations (call centers, data entry, legal research, translation) measuring employment status, earnings, occupational mobility, and wellbeing over 3–5 years. Critical additions: (1) Geographic stratification to detect localized crises. (2) Paired comparison of augmented vs. displaced workers in the same occupation to measure the augmentation-displacement race. (3) Baseline urgency — the speed differential means a 2026 baseline may be the last clean pre-displacement snapshot.
chainNarrative: This chain identifies how AI automation displaces workers at unprecedented speed and scale — targeting cognitive tasks that previously protected white-collar workers — leading to prolonged unemployment and widening economic inequality. Evidence is strong at Step 1 (300M+ jobs exposed globally). The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: historical evidence from trade displacement shows lasting harm, but AI-specific displacement data is sparse, and distributional effects remain speculative.
keyConfounders: Speed of AI capability advancement, labor market tightness, social safety net strength, geographic concentration of displacement, whether displacement is task-level or job-level, policy responses. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Speed differential — AI compresses decades-long manufacturing automation into years for cognitive work, but safety nets were designed for slow transitions. White-collar displaced workers have weaker safety nets than manufacturing workers had (no unions, less geographic concentration, faster skill depreciation). (2) Geographic concentration in knowledge-work hubs creates localized crises that may be masked by aggregate statistics. (3) Direction uncertainty — Brynjolfsson found AI augmentation benefits least-skilled workers most (34% vs. 8%). If augmentation dominates displacement, AI could reduce inequality. No one is measuring the augmentation-displacement race at population scale.

name: AI Augmentation → Productivity Gains → Wage Growth
impactEstimate: ~1.5B knowledge workers globally. If AI augmentation boosts productivity 10-30%, GDP impact could be $2-7T annually. Wage effects depend on labor market structure and bargaining power.
investmentCase: Unfunded in the critical link — AI boosts individual productivity 25–55% (Steps 1-2 heavily funded), but the IT revolution proved gains don't flow to wages without deliberate policy. No one is funding the productivity-wage link because it's not a technology problem — it's a labor market structure problem that AI may worsen by making workers more substitutable.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal study tracking firm-level productivity and worker wage outcomes pre/post AI adoption, controlling for industry and labor market conditions. Critical contingency: if results confirm IT-era decoupling pattern (most likely outcome), pivot from measurement to policy advocacy — the gap shifts from 'Needs Evidence' to 'Needs Policy.'
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI augmentation boosts worker productivity across industries, which then translates into wage growth and shared prosperity. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI tools demonstrably increase individual task performance (55% faster coding, 25% faster consulting tasks). The chain breaks at Steps 3-4: historical precedent shows productivity gains don't automatically flow to wages (the IT revolution saw productivity surge while wages stagnated), and whether AI-era gains will be shared depends on labor market structure and bargaining power, not technology alone.
keyConfounders: Labor market tightness and bargaining power, capital vs. labor share of gains, automation substitution effect, skill-biased technological change, policy and redistribution frameworks. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) IT productivity-wage decoupling IS the null hypothesis — productivity has roughly doubled since 1979 while median real wages grew ~15%. The chain's Step 3 has a direct historical counterexample spanning 50+ years. The burden of proof is on the chain to explain why AI will be different. (2) AI may reduce bargaining power — if AI augmentation gives any worker 55% productivity gains, workers become more substitutable, not less. AI augmentation could paradoxically widen the productivity-wage gap. (3) Composition effects — if firms respond to per-worker productivity gains by hiring fewer workers at the same total output, aggregate wage effects could be zero or negative.

name: Workforce Retraining → Skill Transition → Preserved Employment
impactEstimate: ~400-800M workers potentially affected by AI automation globally. If retraining succeeds for 30-50% of displaced workers, addresses transition for 120-400M people. Current retraining program completion rates are 10-30%.
investmentCase: Unfunded in evidence — historical retraining shows only +5–10% employment effects for slow-moving trade displacement, and AI is 10x faster. Target skill obsolescence means 6-month bootcamps may graduate workers into automated roles. The deepest question: is retraining the right intervention at all, or does AI's speed require fundamentally different models (UBI, wage insurance, portable benefits)?
investableInsight: Fund an RCT comparing four retraining approaches for AI-displaced workers: (1) Bootcamp (6-month intensive). (2) Degree program (2-year). (3) On-the-job AI upskilling (employer-embedded). (4) Adaptive capacity — learning-to-learn approach focused on transferable skills rather than specific technologies. Measure job placement, wage recovery, skill durability at 2 years (are skills still relevant?), and total cost per successful transition. Critical question: does any approach outpace AI's skill obsolescence rate?
chainNarrative: This chain claims workforce retraining programs can successfully upskill AI-displaced workers for new roles, preserving employment. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — significant public and private investment exists but is widely considered insufficient. The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: meta-analyses of historical retraining programs show modest effects at best (+5-10% employment), AI-specific retraining evidence is nearly nonexistent, and rapid AI advancement risks making target skills obsolete before workers complete programs.
keyConfounders: Retraining program completion rates (currently 10-30%), speed of AI capability advancement vs. retraining duration, self-selection bias in programs, employer willingness to hire retrained workers, target skill obsolescence risk. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Target skill obsolescence — AI capability advancement outpaces retraining duration. A 6-month bootcamp may teach skills automated by graduation (prompt engineering emerged 2023, declining by 2025). The retraining treadmill accelerates faster than workers can run. (2) Completion rate crisis — 10–30% completion means 70–90% never finish. This is a program design problem compounded by demographics of displacement (financial stress, family obligations, geographic barriers). (3) Employer skepticism — no evidence employers prefer retrained workers over fresh graduates. The 'retrained displaced worker' may face hiring stigma. (4) Self-selection bias — completers may be those who would have found employment anyway, dramatically overstating program effectiveness.

name: AI Automation → New Industries → Job Creation
impactEstimate: WEF estimated 97M new roles by 2025; partially validated. Historical parallel: IT revolution created ~30M net new US jobs. AI may create 50-150M new roles globally, but composition and accessibility are uncertain.
investmentCase: Unfunded because both sides prefer their priors — new AI-adjacent roles are emerging but the automobile analogy may not hold. Three structural differences (role transience, composition mismatch, geographic concentration) suggest AI job creation follows a different pattern. No real-time observatory tracks net effects.
investableInsight: Fund a real-time labor market observatory tracking: (1) AI-created job categories, skill requirements, wages, and geography. (2) Role longevity — are AI-created roles themselves being automated? Track role creation and destruction rates. (3) Accessibility gap — compare displaced-worker skill profiles against new-role requirements to measure whether new jobs are reachable by displaced populations.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI automation, by eliminating routine tasks, catalyzes new industries and job categories that absorb displaced labor — as automobiles created more jobs than horse-related work they destroyed. Evidence is strong at Step 1 (AI automates tasks) and moderate at Step 2 (new AI-adjacent roles are emerging, e.g., prompt engineers, ML ops). The chain breaks at Step 3: whether net job creation exceeds displacement is unresolved and hotly contested between optimists (WEF, McKinsey) and pessimists (Frey & Osborne, Susskind).
keyConfounders: Speed of displacement vs. creation, skills match between old and new roles, geographic concentration of new industries, accessibility of new roles to displaced workers, policy responses to transition costs. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) AI-created roles may be transient — unlike automobile-era roles that lasted 50+ years, AI-adjacent roles may be automatable within 2–3 years (prompt engineering emerged 2023, already declining). If AI creates and automates roles faster than new ones emerge, net creation is negative. (2) Composition mismatch — new roles require high skill (ML engineering, AI safety, data science) while displacement hits mid-skill (data entry, call centers, paralegal). Displaced workers at Step 1 are not filling roles at Step 2. (3) Geographic concentration — new AI roles cluster in tech hubs (SF, Seattle, NYC, London) while displacement is distributed. The 'same people get new jobs' framing of historical transitions doesn't apply.

name: Literature Synthesis → Research Prioritization → Faster Progress
impactEstimate: ~5M scientific papers published annually; researchers spend ~50% of time on literature review. AI synthesis could reclaim ~$100B+ in researcher time globally and reduce duplicated research (~15% of studies are estimated to unknowingly replicate prior work).
investmentCase: Unfunded because academic incentive structures actively resist AI-optimized research prioritization. Step 1 (synthesis tools saving researcher time) is genuinely useful and worth funding alone. But the chain's escalation to 'faster progress' assumes behavior change that conflicts with publish-or-perish incentives. The gap isn't information — it's incentives.
investableInsight: Commission a head-to-head comparison of AI-identified vs. expert-identified research priorities in 2 fields (one STEM, one social science). Track: (1) Priority quality — do AI-identified priorities produce more impactful results over 2–3 years? (2) Adoption rate — what fraction of researchers actually pursue AI-identified priorities vs. their own interests? (3) Incentive experiment — test whether targeted funding for AI-identified priorities changes adoption rates. This separates 'can AI identify good priorities?' from 'will anyone follow them?' The most investable finding: Step 1 alone (synthesis tools) delivers Moderate impact worth funding on its own merits.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can synthesize scientific literature at scale, identify meaningful research gaps, redirect researcher effort toward high-priority questions, and accelerate overall scientific progress. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — tools like Semantic Scholar and Elicit demonstrate useful synthesis capability, though hallucination rates of 5-15% require human verification. The chain breaks at Steps 3-4: no evidence exists that researchers act on AI-identified priorities, and academic incentive structures (publish-or-perish, novelty bias) may actively work against AI-optimized prioritization.
keyConfounders: Academic incentive structures favoring novelty over gap-filling, funding agency inertia, 5-15% AI hallucination rate in synthesis, researcher autonomy in topic selection, difficulty measuring scientific progress. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Academic incentive structures — publish-or-perish rewards novelty, citation counts, and prolific output, not gap-filling or prioritized research. AI-identified priorities will be ignored if they don't advance careers. (2) Central planner fallacy — the chain assumes science benefits from coordinated prioritization, but many breakthroughs come from serendipity, curiosity-driven research, and interdisciplinary accidents. Over-optimization may reduce diversity of approaches. (3) Statistical vs. strategic gaps — AI can identify what's missing but not what matters. Gap identification requires values, context, and domain judgment. (4) Hallucination contamination — 5–15% hallucination rates mean AI-identified 'gaps' may be artifacts, causing researchers to waste effort on non-existent problems.

name: AI Integrity Tools → Automated Verification → Preserved Scientific Trust
impactEstimate: If AI integrity tools could screen all ~5M papers/year at submission, catching even 50% of fabricated content would prevent ~125K+ compromised papers from entering the literature annually. The leverage is enormous — one tool deployment across major publishers reaches most of global science.
investmentCase: Partially self-correcting at the top: major publishers (Science, MDPI, Wiley, Springer Nature) are adopting AI screening tools, and Springer Nature donated Geppetto to the STM Integrity Hub (April 2025). But most of ~50,000 journals have no screening, preprint channels bypass journals entirely, and no regulatory mandate exists. The arms race with AI text generation is accelerating. The structurally novel gap is preprint screening — underfunded and addressing the actual control point.
investableInsight: The deployment gap is narrowing at major publishers (Science, MDPI, Wiley, Springer Nature self-funding adoption; Geppetto donated to STM Hub April 2025). The remaining investable gaps are: (1) Shared-cost screening infrastructure for the ~50,000-journal long tail ($2-5M). (2) Preprint screening infrastructure — the structurally novel opportunity, addressing the chain-negating preprint bypass (Preprints.ai is the only known initiative, experimental). (3) Regulatory advocacy for mandatory screening requirements. Track 1 (journal deployment) is partially self-correcting through industry. Track 2 (preprint extension + incentive alignment) remains unfunded.
chainNarrative: This chain proposes that AI-powered detection tools can identify fabricated data, images, and text, be integrated into journal workflows, and preserve literature reliability at scale. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — image forensics tools achieve ~85-90% accuracy and text detectors show promise, but no comprehensive tool covers all fabrication types simultaneously. The chain breaks at Step 2: most of the ~50,000 active journals have no automated integrity screening, and publisher incentives (retractions are embarrassing and costly) work against adoption.
keyConfounders: Arms race between fabrication and detection capabilities, publisher resistance to increased retractions, false positive burden on legitimate researchers, cost barriers for small journals, no ground truth dataset of confirmed fabrications for training. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Preprint channels (arXiv, medRxiv, bioRxiv) bypass journal screening entirely and are increasingly the primary dissemination channel — even if all journals adopt screening, fabricated research enters the scientific record through preprints. Preprints.ai is an early experimental initiative but nascent. (2) AI text generation is advancing faster than detection; paper mills operate as organized enterprises that adapt to detection methods — the arms race may already be lost for text-based fabrication. Nature reported dramatic increase in AI-generated text in submissions. (3) The chain assumes journals are the control point for scientific trust, but the landscape is shifting toward preprints and open access, weakening the journal-as-gatekeeper model. (4) NEW: US federal funding cuts to NIH Office of Research Integrity and related oversight — institutional enforcement infrastructure shrinking while the problem grows.

name: AI Fabrication Tools → Unreliable Literature → Erosion of Scientific Trust
impactEstimate: Public trust in science declined from 73% to 57% (Pew 2024). If AI-enabled fabrication further degrades literature reliability, consequences compound: clinical guidelines built on fabricated evidence, policy decisions based on unreliable meta-analyses, reduced public willingness to follow scientific recommendations.
investmentCase: Unfunded in monitoring — AI fabrication is industrializing via paper mills while peer review detection hasn't adapted. The arms race has structural asymmetry: fabrication is cheaper and faster than detection. The preprint bypass means harm occurs before peer review engages. No one knows the current contamination rate.
investableInsight: Fund three parallel tracks: (1) Detection rate study — measure peer review catch rates for AI-fabricated vs. traditionally-fabricated submissions across journal tiers. (2) Literature contamination audit — systematic screening of recent publications (2023–2026) in high-impact journals to estimate current contamination rates. (3) Preprint monitoring — real-time screening of preprint servers (bioRxiv, medRxiv, arXiv) for AI fabrication indicators before findings enter the policy/media cycle. The most actionable first step: no one knows the current contamination rate.
chainNarrative: This chain identifies how AI lowers the cost and skill required to fabricate scientific data, potentially overwhelming peer review and eroding public trust in research. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI can generate realistic microscopy images, Western blots, and entire manuscripts, and paper mills have industrialized this capability. The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: no systematic measurement of whether peer review catch rates have changed post-AI, and no evidence yet linking AI fabrication specifically to trust erosion (though the replication crisis precedent shows the mechanism is plausible).
keyConfounders: Political polarization independently affecting science trust, peer reviewer burnout and declining review quality, difficulty distinguishing AI-fabricated from AI-assisted legitimate work, media amplification of retractions, multi-causal nature of trust erosion. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Preprint bypass — fabricated work spreads through preprints, media, and policy citations before peer review engages. The gatekeeping model is increasingly irrelevant in a preprint-first world. (2) AI-assisted vs. AI-fabricated indistinguishability — as legitimate AI use grows, detection tools will generate massive false positives, undermining the detection system itself. (3) Self-correcting science assumption fails — many fields have low replication rates and high citation inertia; fabricated findings can persist for decades. (4) Political weaponization — revelations of AI fabrication will be amplified by actors who benefit from undermining science trust. The harm of knowing about fabrication may exceed the harm of fabrication itself.

name: Accelerated Discovery → Novel Breakthroughs → Societal Impact
impactEstimate: Science is a ~$2.4T global enterprise. AI-accelerated discovery could reshape entire fields — protein biology (200M+ structures predicted by AlphaFold), materials science (~30K new candidate materials identified), mathematics (new conjectures proven).
investmentCase: Massively funded at discovery ($30B+ DeepMind/Meta R&D) and translation is now attracting major VC ($850M+ autonomous lab startups in 2025), but no one is measuring whether AI compresses the science-to-impact timeline. The investable gap has migrated downstream to measurement.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal tracking study comparing time-to-translation, discovery yield rate, and application quality for AI-origin vs. traditionally-origin discoveries across drug development, materials science, and mathematics. The translation bottleneck itself is no longer underinvested ($850M+ in autonomous lab VC from Lila Sciences, Periodic Labs) — but no one is measuring whether this capital is producing results. The investable gap has migrated from 'fund translation' to 'fund measurement.'
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can identify patterns beyond human capacity, generate novel validated discoveries, translate them into applications, and produce measurable societal benefit. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AlphaFold predicted 200M+ protein structures, GNoME identified 380K new materials, and FunSearch produced novel mathematical results. The chain weakens sharply from Step 3: no AI-discovered drug has been approved, no AI-discovered material has reached commercial scale, and no framework exists to measure whether AI is actually compressing the discovery-to-application timeline.
keyConfounders: 10-30 year discovery-to-application timeline, regulatory and manufacturing bottlenecks unchanged by AI, unknown ratio of AI-generated hypotheses to validated discoveries, publication bias toward AI success stories, difficulty measuring scientific progress. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) AI discoveries may be the wrong kind of novel — AI excels at finding patterns in existing data but may miss creative leaps that drive paradigm shifts. AlphaFold predicted structures from known principles; it didn't discover new physics. (2) Volume ≠ value — AI generates massive candidate volumes (380K materials, 200M structures) but the ratio to useful applications is unknown, potentially overwhelming validation infrastructure. (3) Discovery ecosystem distortion — heavy AI investment in pattern recognition may redirect scientific funding away from hypothesis-driven research and experimental validation. (4) Autonomous lab capacity unproven — $850M+ in VC (Lila Sciences, Periodic Labs) building autonomous synthesis platforms, but labs still setting up as of late 2025; capital committed ≠ infrastructure operational.

name: AI Simulation → Reduced Costs → Democratized Research
impactEstimate: Global R&D spending is ~$2.4T/year. Experimental costs are a major barrier — a single drug candidate costs ~$2.6B to develop, materials characterization can cost $10K-100K per compound. AI simulation could reduce experimental screening costs by 50-90% in some domains, potentially redirecting hundreds of billions toward actual discovery.
investmentCase: Unfunded in verification — corporate claims of 30–70% cost reduction are self-reported without independent audit, and compute costs are rarely factored in. The 'democratization' claim may be backwards: AI simulation replaces one cost barrier (wet lab) with another (compute) that under-resourced institutions may find higher.
investableInsight: Fund an independent, controlled comparison of AI-augmented vs. traditional experimental workflows in 3 domains (one physics-heavy, one biology, one chemistry). Full cost accounting must include: GPU compute, data curation, ML expertise, and validation experiments. Equity dimension: compare total costs for a well-resourced institution (has GPU cluster, ML team) vs. an under-resourced institution (must rent cloud compute, hire consultants). This study directly tests whether AI simulation democratizes or concentrates research capacity.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI simulation can reduce experimental costs, enabling participation from under-resourced institutions and producing discoveries that wouldn't otherwise occur. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — AI simulations achieve useful accuracy in physics-heavy domains (AlphaFold, weather models) but are much weaker in complex biological and social systems. The chain breaks at Step 2: corporate claims of 30-70% cost reduction are self-reported without independent verification, and compute costs of AI simulation itself are rarely factored into comparisons. The equity step (Step 3) is unmeasured — AI tools may widen rather than narrow the research capacity gap.
keyConfounders: Domain-dependent accuracy (strong in physics, weak in complex biology), compute cost rarely included in savings claims, access barriers beyond tool availability (GPU compute, expertise, training data), potential to widen rather than narrow institutional inequality. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Compute cost substitution — AI simulation replaces experimental costs with compute costs ($10K–$1M+ per project). The 'cost reduction' may be a cost shift that benefits compute-rich institutions and disadvantages others. No independent cost accounting exists. (2) Democratization reversal — AI simulation requires GPU compute, ML expertise, curated training data, and institutional support — barriers that may be higher for Global South institutions than traditional wet lab costs. (3) Domain-dependent accuracy — transformative in physics-constrained systems but unreliable in complex biology and useless in social systems. The chain generalizes from AI's strongest domains to all science. (4) Validation bottleneck — AI predictions still require experimental validation; if validation is rate-limiting, net acceleration is modest.

name: Data Center Growth → Grid Strain → Delayed Decarbonization
impactEstimate: IEA projects global data center electricity consumption could double by 2030. US data center electricity could triple by 2028. If this growth is met primarily by fossil fuels, it could add 24-44 Mt CO2/year by 2030 and delay grid decarbonization timelines by years.
investmentCase: Unmeasured because no one is tracking the net balance — $200B+/year in hyperscaler capex drives demand while ~$10M in academic modeling tries to measure the consequences. Funders can't price the decarbonization risk because the most basic accounting hasn't been done.
investableInsight: Fund a comprehensive net-impact accounting framework that measures the total emissions balance of AI infrastructure: new fossil capacity built for data centers, renewable PPA additionality (physical delivery vs. RECs), grid displacement effects, and carbon lock-in risk assessment. The deliverable is a single dashboard answering: 'Is AI infrastructure growth net-positive or net-negative for grid decarbonization?' — giving policymakers and investors an evidence base that currently does not exist.
chainNarrative: This chain identifies how exponentially growing AI compute demand drives new fossil fuel infrastructure that locks in emissions for decades. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — data center electricity demand is growing ~30% annually, already 4.4% of US consumption. The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: while new gas plants are being built for data centers, tech companies are also signing large renewable PPAs, and the net balance between clean and dirty capacity varies by region and remains unmeasured.
keyConfounders: Tech company renewable PPA additionality, SMR and long-duration storage deployment timeline, regional grid mix variation, corporate vs. actual energy sourcing (RECs vs. physical delivery), policy environment for fossil fuel retirement. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) AI IS the harm — this chain is unique because the technology being assessed is the direct cause of the problem, not a potential solution. Every step is driven by AI compute demand, and at no step does AI offer a fix. (2) Demand elasticity from cost reductions — as inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022, usage scales faster than infrastructure projections. Current IEA demand projections may be conservative. (3) The net emissions balance is unknown — $200B+/year creates the demand while ~$10M in academic modeling tries to measure the consequences. No entity is tracking whether total AI infrastructure is net-positive or net-negative for decarbonization.

name: Efficiency Standards → Reduced Compute → Sustainable AI
impactEstimate: If efficiency improvements continue at historical rates (10x per ~2 years for inference), AI energy growth could be partially decoupled from capability growth. But Jevons paradox risk: cheaper compute → more compute used.
investmentCase: Unfunded because no one is building the regulatory infrastructure — the companies that benefit from efficiency also benefit from unconstrained demand growth. Without mandatory standards, Jevons paradox will consume every hardware gain, as it already has since 2022.
investableInsight: Fund development of mandatory AI efficiency standards and per-model carbon labeling (modeled on EPA appliance ratings), plus enforcement mechanism design that accounts for open-weight model proliferation. The regulatory framework must include: (1) mandatory per-model energy reporting for cloud providers, (2) efficiency floor standards that ratchet up over time, (3) international coordination to prevent compute migration to unregulated jurisdictions. Without enforcement infrastructure, voluntary standards will not constrain demand growth.
chainNarrative: This chain proposes that hardware/algorithmic efficiency gains, combined with industry standards and carbon accounting, can constrain AI energy growth within renewable supply limits. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — GPU efficiency improves ~10x per generation and model distillation reduces costs 5-10x. The chain breaks at Step 2: no industry-wide efficiency standards exist, no major cloud provider publishes per-model energy data, and historical precedent (Jevons paradox) suggests efficiency without constraints is consumed by demand growth.
keyConfounders: Jevons paradox (efficiency → increased usage), industry resistance to mandatory standards, competitive dynamics preventing transparency, rapid model architecture changes outpacing standards, global coordination difficulty. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Structural conflict of interest — the companies producing efficiency gains are the same companies driving demand growth. Efficiency is commercially motivated (cheaper compute = more customers), not climate-motivated. Without mandatory constraints, every efficiency improvement enables more usage, not less. (2) Open-weight model proliferation — as efficient models become open-source and run on personal hardware, enforcement of efficiency standards becomes impossible. You can't regulate compute that's distributed across millions of devices. (3) Jevons paradox is not theoretical here — it's already documented. Inference costs dropped 280-fold since Nov 2022, and AI usage has scaled massively. The efficiency gains that would constrain growth are the same gains that fuel it.

name: Materials Discovery → Clean Energy Tech → Accelerated Transition
impactEstimate: Global clean energy investment ~$1.8T/year (IEA 2024). Materials bottlenecks (lithium, cobalt, rare earths) constrain scaling. AI-discovered alternatives could redirect $100B+ in materials supply chains and accelerate deployment timelines by years.
investmentCase: The synthesis bottleneck is real — 380K AI-predicted materials, ~700 tested, zero commercialized. But $1.5B+ in VC and government investment is already targeting this (Lila Sciences $550M, Periodic Labs $300M, ARPA-E $100M+, DOE $320M). The structural pattern (AI accelerates the cheapest step) is correct and cross-domain. This validates the framework's diagnostic power rather than surfacing a novel investment opportunity.
investableInsight: Framework validation example. The synthesis bottleneck (Step 2) is real and the structural pattern mirrors Drug Discovery — AI accelerates the cheapest step. However, $1.5B+ in commercial and government investment is already targeting autonomous synthesis infrastructure. The remaining genuine gaps are at Steps 3–4: no commercialization pathway specifically for AI-discovered clean energy materials, and no measurement of AI's contribution to energy transition. The framework's value here is cross-domain pattern recognition, not discovery of a hidden opportunity.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can accelerate discovery of superior materials for batteries, solar cells, and carbon capture, redirecting $100B+ in supply chains and compressing the clean energy transition timeline. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI screens millions of candidates in hours (GNoME: 380,000 new materials, PNNL: 32M candidates in 80 hours). The chain breaks sharply at Step 2: of 380,000 GNoME materials, only ~700 have been synthesized, and no AI-discovered material has reached commercial production for clean energy.
keyConfounders: 10-20 year materials-to-market timeline, manufacturing scale-up challenges, supply chain dependencies, competing incumbent materials with established production, regulatory approval for new materials. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Valley of death is physics, not computation — AI can screen millions of candidates in hours but can't shortcut synthesis, testing, and manufacturing. Of 380K GNoME materials, only ~700 have been synthesized, zero commercialized. This is the same structural pattern as Drug Discovery: AI accelerates the cheapest, fastest step while the real cost and time is downstream. (2) Incumbent materials keep improving — lithium-ion batteries and silicon solar cells continue to improve through incremental optimization. By the time AI-discovered materials are ready for market (10-20 years), incumbents may have closed the performance gap, reducing the value of novel materials. (3) Volume ≠ value — AI generates massive candidate volumes (380K materials) but the ratio to useful applications is unknown. The bottleneck may shift from 'not enough discoveries' to 'too many candidates to validate,' overwhelming already-limited synthesis infrastructure.

name: Emissions Monitoring → Accountability → Better Policy
impactEstimate: Global GHG emissions ~55 Gt CO2e/year. Climate TRACE now monitors 352 million+ assets globally. Accurate facility-level monitoring could improve emissions accounting for ~80% of global emissions currently estimated rather than measured.
investmentCase: The monitoring-to-consequences pipeline IS being built (EU CBAM, EPA $1.36B methane program) — but on self-reported data, not AI-derived independent monitoring. Climate TRACE sees 352M+ assets but enforcement mechanisms bypass it. The investment: integrate independent monitoring before self-reporting locks in as default.
investableInsight: Fund integration of independent AI-derived emissions data (Climate TRACE, GHGSat, MethaneSAT) into existing and emerging regulatory compliance systems: (1) verified data feeds from Climate TRACE into EU CBAM compliance platforms (Muir AI, Mavarick, etc.) to supplement/replace self-reported producer data, (2) regulatory pilots in 3–5 jurisdictions accepting AI satellite data as primary enforcement evidence, (3) interoperability standards between independent monitoring and regulatory reporting frameworks. The bottleneck is no longer "no consequences pipeline" — it's "the consequences pipeline bypasses independent monitoring."
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-powered satellite monitoring can detect facility-level emissions globally, enable attribution to specific actors, and drive evidence-based climate policy. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — Climate TRACE monitors 352M+ assets across 250 countries. The chain weakens significantly at Steps 3-4: no documented case exists where AI-derived emissions data directly triggered regulatory enforcement. Critical 3a finding: the monitoring-to-consequences pipeline IS being built (EU CBAM compliance startups, EPA $1.36B methane program) but uses self-reported data, not independent AI monitoring — the chain's core technology is being bypassed.
keyConfounders: Political will and regulatory capacity, self-reporting industry resistance, diffuse source attribution difficulty (agriculture, transport), international coordination challenges, legal frameworks lagging technology. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Data-to-action gap is structural, not informational — we've had clear climate emissions data for decades; the bottleneck is political economy, not information quality. (2) Industry capture of monitoring standards — incumbents shape monitoring frameworks to their advantage. (3) Attribution diffusion — systemic reductions require coordinated multi-sector action monitoring alone can't drive. (4) Regulatory bypass of independent monitoring — EU CBAM and EPA methane enforcement build consequences infrastructure on self-reported data, not AI-derived monitoring. The enforcement mechanisms this chain claims AI will enable are being built without it.

name: Grid Optimization → Reduced Emissions → Climate Impact
impactEstimate: Global power sector emits ~15 Gt CO2/year. IEA estimates AI-optimized grids could reduce power sector emissions 5-10% by 2030, ~0.75-1.5 Gt CO2/year addressable.
investmentCase: Heavily funded at the technology layer (Google/DeepMind, Siemens, $470B global grid investment) but no country can attribute measurable emissions reductions to AI grid optimization — and AI's own energy demand may offset the savings.
investableInsight: Fund net-impact accounting infrastructure (~$5-15M) measuring AI grid optimization benefits vs. AI's own energy demand growth — cross-cuts with Data Center Growth ToC. FERC sandbox idea weaker than assessed — FERC 2025-26 agenda focuses on data center demand accommodation, not AI dispatch.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-optimized power grids can reduce global power sector emissions (~15 Gt CO2/year) by 5-10% through better renewable forecasting, reduced curtailment, and displacement of fossil fuel peaker plants. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI forecasting of renewable generation outperforms traditional methods. The chain weakens from Step 2 onward: pilot-level evidence for curtailment reduction exists but no system-wide deployment, and no country has yet attributed measurable emissions reductions to AI grid optimization.
keyConfounders: CHAIN-NEGATING: AI's own energy demand (IEA projects data center demand doubling to ~945 TWh by 2030; AI driving 35-50% of data center power by 2030) may offset or exceed grid optimization savings — no one is measuring the net effect. ALTERNATIVE CAUSE: Battery storage economics (45 GW by end 2026, economic crossover with gas peakers at 4-hr duration) is displacing peakers through cost curves and IRA policy, not AI dispatch — AI's marginal contribution unmeasured. REGULATORY: FERC 2025-2026 reforms enable AI energy demand (data center interconnection), not AI grid dispatch. Utility incumbent resistance to peaker retirement. Attribution structurally impossible — national emissions shaped by renewable buildout, coal retirement, EVs, weather, economic cycles.

name: AI Assessment → Faster Feedback → Improved Learning
impactEstimate: ~69M teacher shortage globally (UNESCO). Teachers spend ~30–40% of time on assessment and grading. AI assessment could free ~10–15 hours/week per teacher for instruction. ~1.6B students could receive more frequent, personalized feedback.
investmentCase: Lightly funded but evidence-blind — no RCT has compared AI-generated vs. expert teacher feedback on actual learning outcomes, so investment is chasing speed without proof it improves learning.
investableInsight: Fund RCTs comparing AI-generated feedback vs. expert teacher feedback on learning outcomes across subjects, measuring both learning gains and total teacher workload impact.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI assessment can free teachers from grading (~30–40% of their time) and provide faster, more frequent feedback to ~1.6B students. Evidence is strong at Step 2 — speed advantage is inherent to the technology — and moderate across other steps. The chain weakens at Steps 3–4: education research shows feedback quality matters more than speed, and AI assessment may create new work (auditing AI grades, handling edge cases) rather than reducing total teacher workload.
keyConfounders: Feedback quality vs. speed tradeoff, AI inability to assess creative and open-ended work, teacher workload shift rather than reduction, student trust in AI grading, assessment gaming. CHAIN-NEGATING: Pedagogical shift toward competencies AI can't assess (creativity, critical thinking, collaboration) — AI may be optimizing for an assessment paradigm being abandoned.

name: Assessment Redesign → AI-Resistant Evaluation → Preserved Learning Integrity
impactEstimate: If adopted broadly across ~220M higher education students and ~1.4B K-12 students, assessment redesign could preserve the credentialing function of education in the AI era. The alternative — an arms race between AI cheating and AI detection — is almost certainly a losing strategy.
investmentCase: Unfunded because institutions are redesigning assessments ad hoc with no shared frameworks, no validity evidence, and no coordination. A solvable coordination problem — but the deeper question is whether assessment redesign is a durable solution or a treadmill, given AI's pace of advancement.
investableInsight: Fund three parallel tracks: (1) Open-source AI-resistant assessment frameworks with instructor training programs ($3-5M). (2) Comparative validity studies testing whether redesigned assessments actually measure the competencies they claim to in AI-available contexts. (3) Longitudinal tracking of whether redesigned assessments maintain resistance as AI capabilities evolve — to test whether this is a durable solution or a treadmill.
chainNarrative: This chain claims redesigning assessments to be AI-resistant or AI-inclusive can preserve the credentialing function of education. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — multiple universities have shifted to oral exams, process portfolios, and in-class demonstrations. The chain weakens at Steps 2–3: no evidence that redesigned formats reliably measure competence in AI-available contexts, and scaling adoption faces major barriers in instructor capacity, institutional inertia, and resource inequality.
keyConfounders: Instructor capacity and training gaps, resource inequality across institutions, AI-resistant formats may test different competencies than intended, partial adoption creating uneven credentialing, equity concerns with oral/in-person formats for students with disabilities. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) AI capability is a moving target — assessments designed to be 'AI-resistant' today may become vulnerable tomorrow as AI advances. The chain assumes a static threat, but AI capabilities change faster than curricula can adapt. Assessment redesign is a treadmill, not a solution. (2) Students adapt faster than institutions — if AI-resistant formats are harder or less convenient, students will find workarounds. Academic integrity history shows students consistently outpace institutional countermeasures. (3) The chain may be solving the wrong problem — maybe education needs to change what it teaches (AI literacy, collaboration with AI) rather than how it tests. Assessment redesign may preserve a credentialing model that's already becoming obsolete in an AI-available world.

name: AI Literacy → Productive Use → Preserved Learning
impactEstimate: If effective AI literacy reaches 50% of ~220M higher education students globally, it could shift norms from dishonesty to productive augmentation, preserving learning outcomes for hundreds of millions.
investmentCase: Unfunded because universities are deploying AI literacy curricula based on anecdotes — zero RCTs test whether training actually changes how students use AI outside the classroom.
investableInsight: Fund multi-institution RCTs comparing learning outcomes for students receiving structured AI literacy training vs. standard instruction, measuring both AI use behavior and knowledge retention over 2+ semesters.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI literacy curricula can shift student behavior from dishonest shortcuts to productive augmentation, preserving learning outcomes. Evidence is entirely speculative — early anecdotal reports from Wharton and Stanford suggest AI-literate students perform better, but no RCTs exist. The critical behavioral question — whether teaching about AI actually changes how students use it outside the classroom — remains unanswered.
keyConfounders: Student motivation and compliance outside classroom settings, rapid AI capability changes outpacing curricula, institutional variation in implementation quality, difficulty measuring productive vs. shortcut AI use. CHAIN-NEGATING: Student incentive structures override literacy training — grade pressure, time constraints, and peer norms favor AI shortcuts regardless of awareness (drug education paradox).

name: AI Tutoring → Personalized Learning → Better Outcomes
impactEstimate: ~1.6B students in primary/secondary education globally. Bloom's 2-sigma problem (1984) showed 1-on-1 tutoring produces 2 SD improvement — AI could democratize this at near-zero marginal cost. Market projected at $6B+ by 2030 but societal value is orders of magnitude larger.
investmentCase: Billions flow into building AI tutors but almost nothing into validating whether they work long-term or match human tutoring quality. The 0.2–0.6 SD evidence base is real but short-term and mostly math/reading — the gap between product investment (Heavy) and independent efficacy research is stark. Vendor-sponsored research pushes Step 2 to Moderate but has conflict-of-interest limitations. The real opportunity: fund measurement infrastructure into platforms that already have millions of users, and start the longitudinal clock now.
investableInsight: Don't wait for time to pass — fund the measurement infrastructure that makes time productive. Three accelerable investments: (1) Embed longitudinal tracking into existing platforms (Khanmigo, Duolingo, Carnegie Learning) that already have millions of users — start the clock now instead of designing studies from scratch. (2) Fund engagement trajectory and AI dependency studies (6–18 months) that can produce go/no-go signals on Steps 3–4 without waiting for full longitudinal outcomes. (3) Fund parallel cross-subject and cross-context pilots (science, writing, social studies; adult ed, non-English, low-resource settings) to compress a 10-year sequential evidence-building process into 2–3 years. All studies must use active comparison groups (good human instruction) and measure metacognition/productive struggle alongside test scores.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI tutoring can democratize personalized instruction for ~1.6B students, addressing Bloom's 2-sigma problem at near-zero marginal cost. Evidence is strongest at the endpoints — AI tutors deliver measurable learning gains (0.2–0.6 SD in meta-analyses) and education quality reliably predicts life outcomes (Chetty, Heckman). The chain weakens in the middle: no longitudinal evidence that AI tutoring gains persist or transfer, and most evidence is concentrated in math/reading for K-12.
keyConfounders: Implementation quality variation, student motivation and engagement decay, digital divide in device/internet access, cultural and linguistic adaptation gaps, teacher displacement effects. CHAIN-LEVEL: (1) AI dependency / skill erosion — the AI Dependency ToC in this same map documents the risk that AI tutoring may undermine independent learning skills, metacognition, and productive struggle. The treatment itself may create a countervailing harm that the chain doesn't acknowledge. (2) Engagement decay may be structural, not incidental — gamification and novelty effects wear off, and AI tutoring may have a natural effectiveness ceiling that degrades over time, potentially negating Step 3 (persistence) entirely. (3) Comparison group problem — most evidence compares AI tutoring to NO tutoring, not to good human tutoring. The chain invokes Bloom's 2-sigma (human 1:1 tutoring = 2 SD) but AI achieves 0.2–0.6 SD, a fraction. The implicit claim that AI approaches human tutoring at scale is unsupported.

name: Assessment Bias → Placement Disparity → Compounding Inequality
impactEstimate: Millions of students annually subject to AI-assisted placement decisions (course tracking, gifted program selection, college admissions). If bias mirrors known AES patterns, underrepresented students could be systematically tracked into lower-opportunity pathways.
investmentCase: Unfunded because known AI scoring biases against AAVE and non-standard English have never been traced to actual placement disparities — plausible harm with zero measurement.
investableInsight: Fund an audit study comparing AI-assisted vs. human-only placement decisions across diverse school districts, tracking whether AI scoring biases translate to measurably different course placements and opportunity access.
chainNarrative: This chain projects that known AI assessment bias (against AAVE, non-standard English, underrepresented demographics) leads to systematically lower course placements, reduced access to advanced courses, and worse life outcomes. The compounding mechanism from traditional tracking research is well-established (Oakes 1985, Domina 2017), but no study has connected AI-driven scoring bias to actual placement disparities or downstream outcomes.
keyConfounders: Pre-existing human tracking biases AI may replicate or amplify, variation in how AI scores inform actual decisions, institutional policies that buffer or magnify bias, socioeconomic confounders. CHAIN-NEGATING: AI may be LESS biased than human decision-makers it replaces — human placement decisions are subjective, inconsistent, and shaped by race/class/appearance. The relevant comparison is AI vs. human status quo, not AI vs. perfection.

name: AI Access → Reduced Inequality → Greater Equity
impactEstimate: ~260M children out of school globally (UNESCO). ~617M children and adolescents lack minimum proficiency in reading and math. ~3.7B people still offline. AI-extended education could address hundreds of millions in LMICs where teacher-to-student ratios exceed 1:60.
investmentCase: Unfunded because existing deployments cluster in well-resourced schools while 260M children remain out of school. No funder is testing whether AI tutoring works for truly unreached learners — and historical edtech precedent (OLPC, MOOCs) suggests technology consistently widens gaps before narrowing them.
investableInsight: Fund equity-focused measurement alongside existing deployment efforts (Gates+ADQ, J-PAL). Specifically: (1) RCTs comparing AI-only vs. AI+human-facilitator models in teacher-shortage regions; (2) longitudinal tracking of whether AI tools close or widen proficiency gaps by income, gender, and geography; (3) explicit measurement of whether tools reach out-of-school children vs. only in-school underperformers. The 'underinvested' framing is correct — unlike other chains, there is no hidden commercial capital flow here. The market failure is real.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can extend quality education to the ~260M children out of school and ~617M lacking basic proficiency by running on low-cost devices in underserved settings. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — tools like Eneza Education and M-Shule already serve millions via SMS. The chain breaks at Steps 2–4: AI tools are English-centric with degraded multilingual performance, deployments cluster in well-resourced schools rather than teacher-shortage areas, and historical edtech (OLPC, MOOCs) largely failed to close equity gaps.
keyConfounders: Digital divide and infrastructure barriers, English-centric AI performance, cultural relevance of content, historical edtech adoption patterns favoring well-resourced learners, device and connectivity costs. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) OLPC/MOOC precedent may be structural, not incidental — historical edtech has consistently widened rather than narrowed equity gaps. Tools designed for well-resourced contexts get adopted first by well-resourced learners. This may be an iron law of technology adoption that AI doesn't change. (2) Out-of-school children are out of school for non-educational reasons — the 260M out-of-school children face poverty, child labor, conflict, disability, and gender barriers. AI tutoring doesn't address any of these root causes. The chain assumes the barrier is lack of teachers, but the barrier is often lack of safety, food, or family permission.
