(3) AI tools could displace human relationships that make education work — in LMIC settings, teachers serve social functions beyond instruction (trust, motivation, safeguarding). Replacing them with AI may lose the social infrastructure that drives educational engagement.

name: Bias Auditing → Compliance → Reduced Discrimination
impactEstimate: Mandatory bias auditing for educational AI used in high-stakes decisions could protect hundreds of millions of students from systematic discrimination in grading, placement, and admissions.
investmentCase: Unfunded because zero educational AI systems have been independently audited — mandates exist in concept but no one is building the audit infrastructure.
investableInsight: Fund independent bias audits of the top 10 educational AI platforms used in high-stakes decisions (grading, placement, admissions), publish results publicly, and track whether disclosure drives vendor improvement.
chainNarrative: This chain claims mandatory bias auditing can compel educational AI vendors to reduce discriminatory impacts, producing more equitable outcomes. Evidence level is None at the terminal step — no educational AI system has been audited, had bias reduced, and then shown improved equity outcomes. The NYC employment AI experience (Local Law 144) suggests mandates change vendor behavior but create enforcement challenges and potential market exits.
keyConfounders: Vendor market exits from regulated jurisdictions, shifting bias to harder-to-detect forms, enforcement capacity gaps, patchwork regulatory landscape, defining fairness metrics for education contexts. CHAIN-NEGATING: Vendor market exits — under-resourced schools dependent on affordable AI tools could be worse off post-regulation if vendors withdraw rather than comply.

name: AI Dependency → Skill Erosion → Workforce Impact
impactEstimate: If widespread AI dependency reduces critical thinking for even 10% of ~1.6B students, long-term workforce adaptability and innovation capacity could decline significantly. Difficult to quantify but analogous to calculator dependency concerns — except AI automates reasoning itself, not just arithmetic.
investmentCase: Unfunded because the harm is slow-moving and invisible — cognitive science predicts AI bypass of productive struggle erodes skills, but zero longitudinal studies exist to confirm or deny it.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal cohort study (5+ years) tracking analytical skill development and workforce outcomes for high-AI-use vs. low-AI-use student populations, starting with current college freshmen.
chainNarrative: This chain projects that student over-reliance on AI will erode critical thinking and independent learning skills, producing graduates with reduced workforce readiness. Evidence is grounded in cognitive science — 'desirable difficulty' theory (Bjork 1994) and productive failure research (Kapur 2008) predict harm from bypassing struggle — but no direct measurement of AI-specific skill erosion exists. The terminal link depends on whether AI-replaced skills remain valued in the workforce.
keyConfounders: AI ubiquity may make replaced skills less relevant, calculator analogy suggests adaptation not decline, individual variation in AI use patterns, workforce skill demands shifting independently of AI education effects. CHAIN-NEGATING: AI may make analytical skills genuinely obsolete — if AI handles analysis universally, individual skill matters less. Counter: evaluating AI outputs requires the very skills AI erodes.

name: Normalized Cheating → Credential Devaluation → Institutional Trust Erosion
impactEstimate: ~1.6B students globally, ~220M in higher education. If AI cheating follows adoption curves of other AI tools (30–60% usage within 2 years), normalization could outpace institutional adaptation. Calculators automated arithmetic; AI automates the reasoning and writing that define educational achievement.
investmentCase: Unfunded because credential erosion is slow and invisible — 30–60% AI usage rates are documented but no one is measuring whether employers are discounting degrees.
investableInsight: Fund employer perception research tracking whether hiring managers are discounting degrees from institutions with known AI integrity challenges, combined with longitudinal tracking of credential signaling value across institution types.
chainNarrative: This chain projects that ineffective AI detection and shifting social norms will normalize AI cheating, devalue educational credentials as competence signals, and destabilize the education-to-employment pipeline. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — Turnitin data shows 11% AI-flagged papers in year 1, surveys suggest 30–60% usage. The downstream credential devaluation and institutional trust erosion are entirely speculative and will take years to manifest.
keyConfounders: Credentials may retain value for socialization and network benefits, elite institutions may maintain credibility through AI-proof assessment, employer hiring practices evolving independently, AI use potentially normalized like calculators. CHAIN-NEGATING: Credential stratification — elite institutions maintain value through expensive AI-proof assessment while non-elite cannot, transforming uniform devaluation into widening inequality.

name: Mental Health AI → Broader Access → Reduced Suffering
impactEstimate: ~1B people globally with mental health conditions (WHO). ~75% in LMICs receive no treatment. In the US, ~160M people live in mental health professional shortage areas.
investmentCase: Investment level unknown despite 1B affected people — AI therapy tools exist but no long-term study shows clinically meaningful improvement, so funders can't assess ROI.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal RCT (12+ months) comparing AI mental health tools vs. waitlist control in mental health professional shortage areas, measuring sustained symptom improvement and whether users are people who genuinely lack access to human therapists.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI chatbots and digital tools can broaden mental health access for the ~1B people with mental health conditions globally, 75% of whom receive no treatment. Evidence is moderate at the endpoints — AI can deliver therapeutic interventions (Woebot, Wysa) and treatment works when received. The chain breaks at Steps 3-4: no long-term evidence that AI tools produce clinically meaningful improvement, and no measurement of whether they actually reduce the treatment gap at population level.
keyConfounders: High user dropout rates (most drop off within 2 weeks), safety concerns (Character.AI suicide case), unclear whether tools reach underserved vs. already-served populations, no long-term follow-up data, regulatory gaps for non-FDA-cleared tools. CHAIN-NEGATING: Retention/dropout — most users abandon mental health apps within 2 weeks. If tools can't retain users long enough to deliver therapeutic benefit, the entire chain collapses.

name: Global Health → Extended Care → Reduced Inequality
impactEstimate: ~4.6B people lack access to essential health services (WHO/World Bank Dec 2025). AI-extended care could reach hundreds of millions in LMICs where there are <1 physician per 1,000 people. ~5M children under 5 die annually from treatable conditions.
investmentCase: Newly funded at the pilot level (Horizon1000 $50M, EVAH $60M in early 2026) but 4.6B people still lack essential health services. Less than 1% of global healthcare AI investment ($14.2B US digital health VC in 2025) targets LMICs. The chain inherits all the Diagnostics chain's structural problems and amplifies them in settings with less infrastructure. 2025-2026 foreign aid cuts compound every barrier.
investableInsight: Fund deployment RCTs measuring health outcomes (not just detection rates) for AI diagnostic tools used by community health workers in LMICs, building on J-PAL's Project AI Evidence and the EVAH initiative. Critically: (1) measure the harm of diagnosis-without-treatment-capacity; (2) measure CHW usability and trust (PATH Rwanda is the first trial); (3) require open-source or locally-owned tool architectures to mitigate technology dependency; (4) measure whether AI-assisted care narrows or widens health inequality between populations served.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI on low-cost hardware can extend healthcare to the 4.6B people lacking essential health services (WHO/World Bank Dec 2025, up from 3.6B) and reduce health inequality. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI diagnostics run on smartphones (Google retinal screening, Butterfly iQ). The chain degrades sharply: deployment remains mostly pilot-stage (Step 2), CHW usability has its first study underway (PATH Rwanda, July 2025) but is still speculative (Step 3), and no one is measuring whether AI-assisted care improves outcomes (Step 4) or reduces inequality (Step 5, zero evidence). New confounders: foreign aid funding instability (Lancet projects 9.4–22.6M additional deaths by 2030 from cuts), and differential AI adoption by wealth ($14.2B US digital health VC vs. $110M LMIC philanthropic) which could make the chain net negative at Step 5.
keyConfounders: Infrastructure barriers (internet, electricity, devices), health system capacity to act on AI findings, community trust in AI tools, training requirements for non-specialist users. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Detection without treatment capacity is harmful — diagnosis without follow-up creates anxiety without benefit and erodes trust. (2) Technology dependency on foreign corporations — Google Health has withdrawn before; neither Horizon1000 nor EVAH requires open-source architectures. (3) Diagnostics chain structural problems amplified in LMIC settings with <1 physician per 1,000 people. (4) Foreign aid funding instability — 2025-2026 restructuring threatens the health systems AI tools would plug into; Lancet projects 9.4-22.6M additional deaths by 2030. (5) Differential AI adoption by wealth — $14.2B US digital health VC vs. $110M LMIC; AI adoption faster in wealthy populations could WIDEN health inequality (IJCAI: AI models induce 2.45-43.2% inequalities for non-White patients).

name: Bias Audit Requirement → Vendor Testing → Reduced Discriminatory Outcomes
impactEstimate: Addresses algorithmic bias harm directly. If bias testing became standard for all 223+ FDA-approved AI devices and future submissions, it could prevent systematic underperformance for underrepresented populations across the entire healthcare AI ecosystem.
investmentCase: Investment level unknown despite emerging regulatory mandates — almost no vendors test demographic performance, and no representative test datasets exist for the top FDA-approved AI tools.
investableInsight: Fund creation of representative demographic test datasets for the top 20 FDA-approved AI diagnostic tools, enabling standardized bias benchmarking that regulators and purchasers can require.
chainNarrative: This mitigation chain proposes mandatory bias audits for healthcare AI tools to drive vendor testing and reduce discriminatory outcomes. Evidence is moderate at Step 1 — regulatory frameworks are emerging (FDA signals, EU AI Act) but not consistently enforced. The chain is weakest at Steps 2-3: almost no vendors voluntarily test or report demographic performance, and no evidence connects bias audits to equitable clinical outcomes.
keyConfounders: Regulatory capture by vendors, checkbox compliance without meaningful testing, representative test dataset availability, enforcement gaps across jurisdictions, cost of curating diverse test data. CHAIN-NEGATING: Checkbox compliance — analogous regulatory regimes (EEO, clinical quality metrics) produce compliance documentation without meaningful behavior change.

name: Algorithmic Bias → Misdiagnosis Disparity → Compounding Health Inequality
impactEstimate: ~3.6B people globally lack access to essential health services. Demographic performance gaps in AI diagnostics risk systematically underserving the populations most in need, compounding existing health disparities for billions.
investmentCase: Investment level unknown and unmeasured — Obermeyer et al. proved algorithmic bias exists, but no one is systematically tracking whether it produces differential clinical outcomes at scale.
investableInsight: Fund a multi-site study measuring clinical outcome differences (diagnosis accuracy, treatment appropriateness, mortality) between demographic groups in facilities using AI diagnostic tools vs. matched controls.
chainNarrative: This chain describes how AI tools trained on non-representative data produce demographic performance gaps that lead to misdiagnosis disparities and compound health inequality over time. Evidence is strongest at Step 1 — algorithmic bias is well-documented (Obermeyer et al. 2019 showed a widely-used algorithm underestimated illness severity for Black patients). The chain weakens at Steps 2-3: no systematic measurement of how AI bias translates to differential clinical outcomes at scale.
keyConfounders: Pre-existing healthcare disparities independent of AI, clinician override of AI recommendations, varying adoption rates across facilities, non-AI sources of diagnostic bias. CHAIN-NEGATING: AI might REDUCE disparities compared to the human baseline — human clinical decision-making is known to be affected by implicit bias. If AI standardizes care (even with some bias), net effect could be positive vs. variable human decisions.

name: Drug Discovery → Faster Development → More Treatments
impactEstimate: Drug development costs ~$2.6B per approved drug; $1.3T global pharma R&D spend. If AI cuts discovery costs by 50%, >$100B redirectable. Could unlock treatments for neglected diseases where economics don't currently justify R&D.
investmentCase: Massively funded at the discovery layer ($1.86B market, $12.3B in M&A) but the chain breaks at the same place every pharma chain breaks — access and incentives. AI makes discovery faster but discovery is only ~15% of total cost, no evidence AI drugs succeed at higher rates, and no mechanism ensures treatments reach underserved populations.
investableInsight: Fund two parallel tracks: (1) A systematic tracking registry comparing clinical trial outcomes for all AI-originated drug candidates vs. matched traditionally-discovered candidates — the first evidence base for whether AI improves trial success rates. (2) Policy analysis of incentive mechanisms (orphan drug credits, advance market commitments, prize-based models) that would redirect AI-driven cost savings toward neglected diseases. The tracking study alone answers Step 2 but doesn't address the access problem at Step 4.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI can accelerate drug discovery, reduce the ~$2.6B per-drug development cost, and deliver more treatments to patients. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI identifies candidates faster (Insilico Medicine reached Phase II in <30 months vs. ~4.5 years industry average). The chain weakens sharply from Step 2: no data on whether AI-discovered drugs succeed in trials at higher rates (sample size still tiny), and pharma incentive structures don't guarantee treatments reach underserved populations.
keyConfounders: Regulatory timelines unchanged by AI, pharma incentive structures favoring profitable diseases, manufacturing scale-up challenges, drug pricing and access barriers, small sample of AI-originated drugs in trials. CHAIN-NEGATING: (1) Discovery is only ~15% of total drug development cost/time — AI accelerates discovery but clinical trials, manufacturing, and regulatory approval (~85% of cost) are unchanged. The chain title implies 'faster development' but AI only delivers faster discovery. (2) AI may optimize for the wrong endpoint — candidate identification optimizes for binding affinity and drug-like properties, but clinical failure is usually due to safety/toxicity issues that emerge in later testing. No evidence AI finds safer or more effective candidates, just faster ones. (3) Pharma will capture efficiency gains — if AI makes discovery cheaper, pharma can develop more drugs OR increase profit margins. Incentive structures strongly favor the latter. The chain assumes cost savings flow to more treatments, but they're more likely to flow to shareholders without policy intervention.

name: Diagnostics → Earlier Detection → Lives Saved
impactEstimate: ~10M cancer deaths/year globally; AI-aided screening could address ~2-3M through earlier detection of breast, lung, colorectal, cervical cancers. 223+ FDA-approved devices already deployed.
investmentCase: Heavily funded at the technology layer (223+ FDA devices, billions in venture) but the chain breaks at infrastructure — no one is funding the system-level changes needed to translate AI detection into earlier treatment for underserved populations.
investableInsight: Fund implementation science pilots that pair AI diagnostic deployment with follow-up navigators, expanded biopsy/treatment capacity, and demographic-stratified performance monitoring in underserved settings. Measure time-to-treatment (not just detection), stratified by population.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI diagnostics can detect disease earlier and save lives, addressing ~2-3M of ~10M annual cancer deaths globally. Evidence is strong at the endpoints — AI reads medical images at clinician-level accuracy (223+ FDA devices) and earlier treatment clearly improves survival (Stage I breast cancer 99% vs. Stage IV 29%). The main gaps are in the middle: whether AI detection actually accelerates diagnosis and treatment in real clinical workflows, and whether performance holds across diverse populations.
keyConfounders: Health system capacity to act on AI findings, insurance and access barriers to follow-up, performance degradation on underrepresented populations, false positive burden, workflow integration challenges, clinicians ignoring/overriding AI flags. CHAIN-LEVEL: The combination of workflow + capacity + access barriers means the middle of the chain is blocked by infrastructure, not evidence. Steps 1–2 deliver Large impact for already-served populations; Transformational impact requires systemic change beyond AI.

name: Genomics / Precision Medicine → Targeted Therapy → Better Outcomes
impactEstimate: Cancer alone: ~19.3M new cases/year globally. Precision oncology improving outcomes for ~30% of patients with actionable mutations. Pharmacogenomics could reduce ~2M serious adverse drug reactions/year in the US.
investmentCase: Lightly funded but equity-blind — 78% of genomic data comes from European-ancestry populations, making AI-derived precision medicine inapplicable to most of the world.
investableInsight: Fund development and validation of AI-derived polygenic risk scores for non-European ancestry populations, addressing the critical representation gap (78% of GWAS participants are European) that limits precision medicine's global reach.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI-powered genomics can identify clinically meaningful patterns leading to targeted therapies with better outcomes. Evidence is strong at the endpoints — AI detects genomic patterns (AlphaFold, DeepVariant) and targeted therapies work where they exist (Herceptin, imatinib). The chain breaks in the middle: most AI-identified genomic associations have small effect sizes and limited clinical utility, and precision medicine outside oncology remains largely aspirational.
keyConfounders: Genomic database ancestry skew, small effect sizes of most genetic variants, lack of genetic counseling infrastructure, cost beyond academic centers, limited clinical utility outside oncology. CHAIN-NEGATING: Oncology concentration limits generalizability — precision medicine works where tumor biology is uniquely amenable to targeted approaches. Extrapolation to other disease areas has been largely unsuccessful.

name: Admin Automation → Lower Costs → Greater Access
impactEstimate: ~$1T/year in US healthcare admin costs (34% of total spend). $200-360B estimated waste. Even a 10% reduction = $20-36B/year freed up — but only if savings pass through to patients.
investmentCase: Unfunded in the critical link — AI admin tools work (Nuance DAX, Abridge) but no evidence exists that cost savings reach patients rather than being absorbed as profit.
investableInsight: Fund a longitudinal study tracking whether AI admin automation savings at health systems translate to lower patient costs or expanded access, comparing AI-adopting vs. non-adopting systems over 3+ years.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI automation of healthcare admin tasks (billing, prior auth, scheduling, documentation) can reduce the ~$1T/year US admin cost burden and improve patient access. Evidence is strong at the endpoints — AI can automate admin tasks (Nuance DAX, Abridge) and access clearly improves outcomes (Medicaid expansion data). The chain breaks at Step 3: no evidence that cost savings pass through to patients rather than being absorbed as profit, consistent with the history of hospital mergers.
keyConfounders: Healthcare market structure absorbing savings as profit, implementation costs offsetting savings, induced demand from efficiency gains, vendor fees, regulatory overhead. CHAIN-NEGATING: Every prior efficiency innovation in US healthcare (hospital mergers, EHR adoption) resulted in savings captured as profit, not passed to patients. No regulatory mechanism forces pass-through of AI savings.

name: Clinical Decision Support → Better Decisions → Better Outcomes
impactEstimate: ~250K preventable deaths/year in the US from medical errors. Globally, WHO estimates 5.2M deaths/year from poor-quality care in LMICs.
investmentCase: Unfunded in workflow integration — AI generates accurate recommendations but 49–96% are overridden due to alert fatigue, and Epic's sepsis model showed zero mortality improvement.
investableInsight: Fund a multi-site RCT comparing patient outcomes (mortality, adverse events) in facilities using AI clinical decision support vs. standard care, with focus on workflow integration approaches that reduce alert fatigue.
chainNarrative: This chain claims AI clinical decision support can reduce medical errors (~250K preventable US deaths/year) by improving clinician recommendations. Evidence is strong at Step 1 — AI generates clinically relevant recommendations (sepsis prediction, drug interactions, Med-PaLM 2). The chain breaks at Steps 2-3: clinicians override 49-96% of alerts due to alert fatigue, and Epic's sepsis model showed no mortality improvement in a rigorous JAMA 2022 evaluation.
keyConfounders: Alert fatigue and clinician override rates, workflow integration quality, confirmation bias in AI adoption, EHR system variation, AI model performance degradation over time. CHAIN-NEGATING: Confirmation bias in AI adoption — clinicians follow AI when it confirms existing judgment and override when it challenges. AI adds most value where least needed, is ignored where it could help most.
